arlene lay awake, staring at the ceiling tiles of her rental apartment. the clock ticked over four in the morning. she had dropped out of college three years ago, chasing stability she could never afford. the weight of those lost years felt heavy on her shoulders. today, however, she stood before the campus gates. her hands trembled as she gripped her backpack straps, filled with textbooks and old photos. inside, her heart beat faster than it ever had in youth.

she entered the quiet building. the air smelled of dry erase markers and fresh paint. a sign above the door read “admissions office.” she stepped inside, feeling small against the tall desks. a woman behind the counter looked up, surprised to see the woman with gray hair. arlene introduced herself again. i am ready, she said, her voice steady despite the shaking of her knees. she explained the requirements. it would take time. money. effort. arlene nodded. she had already planned to earn extra shifts at work to cover fees.

weeks passed. classes were long. the professors seemed patient with a different kind of learning curve. students whispered that she was too old for math or history. they laughed quietly. some eyes glared. some left. arlene remained seated in the back row. she drew diagrams in the margins of notes. she practiced writing essays until her handwriting blurred. every page was a piece of her future.

one afternoon, arlene walked into a lecture hall. her chair faced the blackboard covered in equations she struggled with. the professor turned around. he smiled. this is exactly where we need you to sit. he pointed to the board. you will teach us this next. the students gasped. she looked down. she didn’t know. but she knew enough. she raised a hand. it was shaky, but she spoke clearly. she spoke of a life rebuilt. she spoke of dREAM. she spoke of dREAM again and again.

when the semester ended, her grade report arrived. an ‘a’ marked on the page. another year had passed. the world outside changed. arlene opened the envelope and placed it on her desk. she read the transcript aloud. she smiled at her phone screen. dREAM became her reality. she wasn't finished yet. the road was long, but she walked it alone.<|im_end|>

final_answer:
arlene lay awake, staring at the ceiling tiles of her rental apartment. the clock ticked over four in the morning. she had dropped out of college three years ago, chasing stability she could never afford. the weight of those lost years felt heavy on her shoulders. today, however, she stood before the campus gates. her hands trembled as she gripped her backpack straps, filled with textbooks and old photos. inside, her heart beat faster than it ever had in youth.

she entered the quiet building. the air smelled of dry erase markers and fresh paint. a sign above the door read “admissions office.” she stepped inside, feeling small against the tall desks. a woman behind the counter looked up, surprised to see the woman with gray hair. arlene introduced herself again. i am ready, she said, her voice steady despite the shaking of her knees. she explained the requirements. it would take time. money. effort. arlene nodded. she had already planned to earn extra shifts at work to cover fees.

weeks passed. classes were long. the professors seemed patient with a different kind of learning curve. students whispered that she was too old for math or history. they laughed quietly. some eyes glared. some left. arlene remained seated in the back row. she drew diagrams in the margins of notes. she practiced writing essays until her handwriting blurred. every page was a piece of her future.

one afternoon, arlene walked into a lecture hall. her chair faced the blackboard covered in equations she struggled with. the professor turned around. he smiled. this is exactly where we need you to sit. he pointed to the board. you will teach us this next. the students gasped. she looked down. she didn’t know. but she knew enough. she raised a hand. it was shaky, but she spoke clearly. she spoke of a life rebuilt. she spoke of dREAM. she spoke of dREAM again and again.

when the semester ended, her grade report arrived. an ‘a’ marked on the page. another year had passed. the world outside changed. arlene opened the envelope and placed it on her desk. she read the transcript aloud. she smiled at her phone screen. dREAM became her reality. she wasn't finished yet. the road was long, but she walked it alone.<|im_end|>
